title: Exercises: QFT Properties
description: Practice with key properties of the Quantum Fourier Transform

exercises.title: Verify Unitarity of QFT
exercises.description: Verify that the QFT matrix is unitary by checking that QFT† · QFT = I.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n):
    """Create n-qubit QFT matrix."""
    N = 2**n
    omega = np.exp(2j * np.pi / N)
    return np.array([[omega**(j*k) for k in range(N)] for j in range(N)]) / np.sqrt(N)

# Verify unitarity for 2, 3, 4 qubits
for n in [2, 3, 4]:
    QFT = qft_matrix(n)
    # Your code: check QFT† @ QFT ≈ I
    pass
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n):
    """Create n-qubit QFT matrix."""
    N = 2**n
    omega = np.exp(2j * np.pi / N)
    return np.array([[omega**(j*k) for k in range(N)] for j in range(N)]) / np.sqrt(N)

print("Unitarity Verification: QFT† · QFT = I")
print("=" * 40)

for n in [2, 3, 4]:
    QFT = qft_matrix(n)
    QFT_dag = QFT.conj().T
    product = QFT_dag @ QFT
    identity = np.eye(2**n)
    is_unitary = np.allclose(product, identity)
    print(f"{n} qubits (N={2**n}): Unitary = {is_unitary}")
    
# Also verify QFT @ QFT† = I
print("\nAlso checking QFT · QFT† = I:")
for n in [2, 3]:
    QFT = qft_matrix(n)
    is_unitary = np.allclose(QFT @ QFT.conj().T, np.eye(2**n))
    print(f"{n} qubits: {is_unitary}")

exercises.title: QFT of Computational Basis States
exercises.description: Apply QFT to each computational basis state and observe the resulting superposition patterns.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n):
    N = 2**n
    omega = np.exp(2j * np.pi / N)
    return np.array([[omega**(j*k) for k in range(N)] for j in range(N)]) / np.sqrt(N)

n = 3
N = 2**n
QFT = qft_matrix(n)

# Apply QFT to each basis state |k⟩
for k in range(N):
    basis_state = np.zeros(N)
    basis_state[k] = 1
    # Your code: apply QFT and analyze result
    pass
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n):
    N = 2**n
    omega = np.exp(2j * np.pi / N)
    return np.array([[omega**(j*k) for k in range(N)] for j in range(N)]) / np.sqrt(N)

n = 3
N = 2**n
QFT = qft_matrix(n)

print(f"QFT of computational basis states (n={n} qubits)")
print("=" * 50)
print("\nQFT|k⟩ = (1/√N) Σⱼ ω^(jk)|j⟩")
print("\nKey observation: QFT|0⟩ = uniform superposition")
print("                QFT|k⟩ = superposition with phase pattern\n")

for k in range(N):
    basis_state = np.zeros(N)
    basis_state[k] = 1
    result = QFT @ basis_state
    
    # All amplitudes have same magnitude
    magnitudes = np.abs(result)
    phases = np.angle(result) * 180 / np.pi  # in degrees
    
    print(f"|{k}⟩ = |{k:03b}⟩:")
    print(f"  All |amplitudes| = {magnitudes[0]:.4f} = 1/√{N}")
    print(f"  Phases (deg): {np.round(phases, 1)}")
    print()
hints: QFT|0⟩ gives uniform superposition with all phases = 0
QFT|k⟩ gives phases that vary as j·k·(2π/N)

exercises.title: Period Detection with QFT
exercises.description: Use QFT to detect the period of a periodic state. This is the core of Shor's algorithm!
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n):
    N = 2**n
    omega = np.exp(2j * np.pi / N)
    return np.array([[omega**(j*k) for k in range(N)] for j in range(N)]) / np.sqrt(N)

def create_periodic_state(N, period):
    """Create state with peaks at 0, period, 2*period, ..."""
    state = np.zeros(N)
    for i in range(0, N, period):
        state[i] = 1
    return state / np.linalg.norm(state)

n = 4
N = 2**n
QFT = qft_matrix(n)

print("Period Detection via QFT")
print("="*50)
print("\nKey insight: QFT converts periodic signal to frequency peaks!")
print("Peaks appear at multiples of N/period\n")

for period in [2, 4, 8]:
    periodic = create_periodic_state(N, period)
    transformed = QFT @ periodic
    probs = np.abs(transformed)**2
    
    # Find peaks
    peaks = np.where(probs > 0.01)[0]
    expected_spacing = N // period
    
    print(f"Period = {period}:")
    print(f"  Input peaks at: {list(range(0, N, period))}")
    print(f"  QFT peaks at: {list(peaks)}")
    print(f"  Peak spacing: {expected_spacing} = N/period = {N}/{period}")
    print(f"  → Detected period: N/spacing = {N}/{expected_spacing} = {period} ✓")
    print()
hints: QFT transforms period r in time domain to spacing N/r in frequency domain
This is the key insight behind Shor's algorithm
